content.full_text:
LUZ DEVELOPMENT AND FINANCE CORPORATION

924 Westwood Boulevard
Suite 1000
Los Angeles, CA 90024
213 208-7444
Telex 181515 LUZ LA
Facsimile 213 824-7843

June 1, 1990

Mr. John Rothman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Rothman:

Pursuant to California Government Code Section 83114 (b), Luz
Development & Finance Corporation requests advice from the Fair
Political Practices Commission concerning a solar collector model
which was provided to members of the California Public Utilities
Commission in January of this year. A list of these individuals
is enclosed for your convenience.

This model is an accurate scale depiction of a Luz solar collector.
It is mounted on a wood platform, 19 inches by 7 inches, and it
stands 8 inches high. The model, which was designed from the
blueprints of the actual collectors, shows the key elements of the
collector, including the heat collection element and the
computerized tracking system. The collector segment of the model
moves in a north-south direction, replicating the movement of an
actual collector. The model is made of plastic and glass. The
model cost approximately $300 to produce. The models have no other
functional value, nor do they contain materials which have
significant independent value.

Luz provided the model in an effort to facilitate the
commissioners' understanding of Luz's solar thermal technology.
Luz is the only developer of this technology, and the solar
collector, as depicted in the model, is the primary feature which
distinguishes Luz's technology from other developers. The model
further illustrates the distinction between Luz's LS-3 and LS-2
technology. This advance enabled Luz to significantly increase the
efficiency and lower the cost of solar energy. A diagram of the
Luz system (attached) was included with the model to provide
additional useful information.

The models were provided to the commissioners for this educational
purpose. Because of what Luz believed to be the educational nature
of the models, there was no thought given to how the models might
be regarded. Because individuals at the CPUC have expressed a
concern as to this matter, Luz is requesting your advice at this
time.

Mr. John M. Rothman
June 1, 1990
Page -2-


It would be appreciated if you would advise us of your concurrence
as to the informational nature of these models.  If you have
further questions, please contact our attorney Mr. Donald Clary at
213-443-1218.  Your help in this matter is appreciated.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Theodore C. Cooke
Senior Vice President

TCC:jv

Enclosure

cc:  Anne Mester, CPUC
     Donald M. Clary

California Public Utilities Commission
505 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, California 94102

G. Mitchell Wilk, President

Commissioner Frederick R. Duda

Commissioner Patricia M. Eckert

Commissioner Stanley W. Hulett

Commissioner John B. Ohanian

sections.question: Pursuant to California Government Code Section 83114 (b), Luz Development & Finance Corporation requests advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission concerning a solar collector model which was provided to members of the California Public Utilities Commission in January of this year.
sections.question_synthetic: Luz Development & Finance Corporation seeks FPPC advice regarding whether solar collector scale models (costing approximately $800 to produce) provided to California Public Utilities Commission members constitute permissible educational gifts or materials under campaign finance law.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from Luz Development & Finance Corporation to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section present. Document contains formal question framing under Government Code Section 83114(b). Sender expresses concern about how models 'might be regarded' and requests FPPC concurrence on their informational nature.

embedding.summary: Luz Development & Finance Corporation requests FPPC advice on whether providing scale model solar collectors to California Public Utilities Commission members was permissible, claiming the models were provided for educational purposes to explain Luz's solar thermal technology.
